facts_summary: ASK filed public interest litigation challenging eviction notices issued to slum residents on government land, arguing violation of fundamental rights. Government contended the land was allotted for government office construction and petitioners were unauthorized occupants without legal right to shelter.
statutes_cited: Government and Local Authority Lands and Buildings (Recovery of Possession) Ordinance 1970 Section 5(1); Constitution Articles 27, 31, 32
notes: Rule discharged; right to shelter is non-justiciable and unlawful occupation creates no legitimate expectation against eviction

facts_summary: Dispute over inheritance of land under Hindu Dayabhaga law. Nephew claimed inheritance on grounds he performed funeral rites of deceased uncle after the brother refused to do so. Trial court dismissed suit; appellate court reversed in favor of nephew.
statutes_cited: Dayabhaga school of Hindu Law; inheritance succession
notes: HCD set aside appellate judgment and restored trial court dismissal; brother ranks higher than nephew under Dayabhaga law regardless of funeral rites

facts_summary: Plaintiffs sought declaration of title over land lost to river erosion in 1967 that reappeared in 1974. Government argued land vested in the Government under Section 86 of the State Acquisition and Tenancy Act as amended in 1972 since it reappeared after the cut-off date.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950 Section 86; P.O. No. 135 of 1972
notes: Appeal dismissed; land reappearing after 1972 vests absolutely in Government; plaintiffs' rights were extinguished

facts_summary: Petitioners challenged a District Judge's order in a title execution case that condoned delay and admitted revision but refrained from granting ad-interim stay of execution proceedings. Petitioners argued non-grant of stay amounted to implied refusal.
statutes_cited: Code of Civil Procedure Section 115(4)
notes: Rule made absolute; non-grant of interim stay constitutes implied refusal entitling petitioners to seek revisional remedy

facts_summary: Plaintiff claimed right to repurchase property based on a reconveyance deed executed in 1963 with a repurchase condition valid until 1971. P.O. No. 88 of 1972 came into effect after the repurchase condition had already expired, making the transaction past and closed.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950 Sections 95 and 95A; P.O. No. 88 of 1972; P.O. No. 136 of 1972
notes: Plaintiff not entitled to relief as repurchase condition expired before Presidential Order; transaction was past and closed

facts_summary: Plaintiff filed partition suit claiming share in jointly held land. Defendants challenged plaintiff's title. Court held that when defendants question title in a partition suit the court must assess and determine the plaintiff's title before ordering partition.
statutes_cited: Code of Civil Procedure Section 54; Order 20 Rule 18; Order 26 Rule 13
notes: Remanded for proper adjudication of title and partition together to avoid multiplicity of proceedings

appellant: East West Property Development (Pvt.) Ltd. and another
facts_summary: Petitioner challenged cancellation of property mutation by Deputy Commissioner based on an acquisition proceeding that had become non-est due to repeal of the relevant law. No government decision for valid acquisition of the property had been issued.
statutes_cited: Emergency Requisition of Property Act 1948; স্থাবর সম্পত্তি অধিগ্রহণ ও হুকুমদখল আইন ২০১৭
notes: Property acquisition found invalid; no government decision existed and acquisition proceeding became non-est due to statutory repeal

appellant: Monto Sheikh being dead his legal heirs
respondent: Ibrahim Miah being dead his legal heirs
facts_summary: Plaintiff claimed title and possession over suit land but could not prove title. The basis of the plaintiff's claim was a settlement that had been cancelled with the cancellation order still in existence.
statutes_cited: Evidence Act 1872; Code of Civil Procedure
notes: Plaintiff failed to prove title; cancelled settlement cannot form valid basis of title claim

respondent: Charge Officer and Appeal Officer and others
facts_summary: Petitioners challenged a Settlement Officer who exercised power under Rule 42 to nullify land record proceedings up to the Appellate Division level, reversing the entire course of the parent law's prescribed sequence. Government had not delegated power to reverse parent law proceedings.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Rules 1955 Rule 42; Settlement Officer jurisdiction
notes: Settlement Officer's nullification of parent law proceedings declared a nullity; revenue officers cannot reverse what Parliament prescribed

facts_summary: Petitioners challenged cancellation of their land lease where the authority cancelled the lease first and then called them to appear with valid papers afterward. The principle of natural justice required hearing before decision, not after.
statutes_cited: Principles of natural justice; audi alteram partem
notes: Authority violated natural justice by cancelling lease before affording petitioners opportunity to be heard; hearing must be pre-decisional

facts_summary: Pre-emption case where the pre-emptee claimed protection under Section 96(10) of the State Acquisition and Tenancy Act as a relation within three degrees. Court examined whether a brother-in-law qualified within three degrees of consanguinity under the Succession Act.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950 Section 96(10); Succession Act 1925 Section 28
notes: Pre-emptee not entitled to protection as brother-in-law is not within three degrees of consanguinity; pre-emption protection denied

facts_summary: Plaintiff landlord sought ejectment of defendants who denied the plaintiff's title to the land. Court examined whether notice under Section 106 of the Transfer of Property Act was required when defendants deny the landlord's title.
statutes_cited: Transfer of Property Act Section 106
notes: Defendants forfeited right to stay by denying landlord's title; notice under Section 106 dispensed with when tenant denies title

facts_summary: Plaintiff claimed title over land that diluvated before 1965 and was recorded as government khas land. Government argued land vested under Section 86 of SAT Act. Court examined legal effect of diluvion and government's right to lease reappeared land.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950 Section 86; P.O. No. 135 of 1972
notes: Government acquired lawful right to lease diluvated land; plaintiff's title extinguished after diluvion regardless of prior ownership

facts_summary: Plaintiff sought specific performance of a 1978 agreement to purchase land where he paid Tk. 18,000 earnest money but vendor refused to execute sale deed. Defendants claimed independent title through an earlier registered gift deed from 1972 and alleged the agreement was forged.
statutes_cited: Specific Relief Act Section 25(a); Code of Civil Procedure
notes: Case remanded for expert comparison of thumb impressions and determination of third-party title and possession; plaintiff gets proper adjudication

facts_summary: Garment company sought to restrain LC payments to fabric suppliers alleging fraud in goods supply and substitution of contracted fabrics. Bank's obligation to honor letters of credit was challenged. Court found plaintiff had benefited from goods and raised fraud as an afterthought.
statutes_cited: Letter of Credit principles; UCP
notes: Appeal dismissed; LC payments cannot be restrained as no fraud was established and LC issuing bank cannot be restrained under any circumstances

respondent: Lahmeyer International Pally Power Services Ltd
facts_summary: Judgment debtor challenged restoration of execution case for enforcement of Singapore International Arbitration Centre arbitral award after the execution case was dismissed for default when decree holder failed to appear due to cause list mistake.
statutes_cited: Order 21 Rule 11 CPC; Section 48 CPC; Article 182 Limitation Act; Section 151 CPC
notes: Rule discharged; restoration of execution case valid under inherent powers where dismissal caused by court mistake causing no injustice

respondent: Haji Md. Akkash Ali and another
facts_summary: Plaintiffs sought specific performance of a 2015 land sale agreement and challenged a subsequent sale deed as collusive and forged. Trial court rejected the plaint as barred by limitation since the agreement expired in October 2015 with no timely suit filed.
statutes_cited: Order VII Rule 11(d) CPC; Specific Relief Act Section 21A; Section 151 CPC
notes: Appeal dismissed; plaintiffs lacked legal title and suit barred by limitation and Section 21A of Specific Relief Act

respondent: Md. Rustom Ali Bepari and others
facts_summary: Minors entered a registered contract for land sale where vendor received nearly entire consideration but refused to execute sale deed. Trial court dismissed suit on grounds that minors cannot contract. High Court found suit also barred for failure to deposit balance consideration.
statutes_cited: Contract Act 1872 Section 11; Specific Relief Act 1877 Section 21A(b)
notes: Rule discharged; suit barred under Section 21A(b) of Specific Relief Act for failure to deposit balance consideration at time of filing

appellant: Md. Badaruddin being dead his heirs
facts_summary: Plaintiff sought specific performance of a Bainapatra contract for sale of property. Defendant argued time in arbitration proceedings should extend the limitation period. Court held equity aids the vigilant and specific performance requires prompt filing.
statutes_cited: Limitation Act 1908 Article 113; Specific Relief Act
notes: Time in arbitration did not extend limitation; specific performance denied as plaintiff did not come promptly to court

respondent: Central Procurement Technical Unit (CPTU) and others
facts_summary: Petitioner contractor challenged a Review Panel decision that dismissed their procurement tender appeal without properly considering its merits. The legal question was whether CPTU Review Panel had jurisdiction to allow an appeal or only dismiss it.
statutes_cited: Public Procurement Rules; CPTU Review Panel jurisdiction
notes: Review Panel has jurisdiction to allow appeals as well as dismiss them; dismissal without proper consideration was improper

facts_summary: Petitioner challenged Review Panel decision in a procurement tender case where the Panel exceeded its jurisdiction under PPR Rule 60 and the authority refused to issue a No Objection Certificate despite the petitioner winning the tender.
statutes_cited: Public Procurement Rules 2008 Rule 60; CPTU jurisdiction
notes: Review Panel exceeded jurisdiction; failure to issue NOC found manifestly arbitrary without lawful authority; petitioner entitled to NOC

appellant: Shetu International Pvt. Ltd and others
respondent: Artha Rin Adalat-2, Dhaka and another
facts_summary: Petitioner challenged an Artha Rin court order where the bank published summons in newspaper without a court order and without first attempting service through process server or post, contrary to mandatory procedure under Section 7(1) of the Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 6(2) and 7(1)
notes: Newspaper publication without prior court order found contrary to mandatory procedural requirements; bank cannot take unilateral steps

facts_summary: Petitioner participated in a government tender for land, paid earnest money as accepted by the authority, but the government attempted to change the valuation and terms after the tender was accepted. Court examined basic principles of offer and acceptance in contract law.
statutes_cited: Contract Act 1872; principles of offer and acceptance; legitimate expectation
notes: Government bound by tender offer once accepted with payment; sale binding from moment tender conditions complied with

facts_summary: Artha Rin suit where the borrower sought court intervention to exempt him from paying high interest on a bank loan. Court examined whether Artha Rin courts had power to exempt defendants from interest liability under the Artha Rin Adalat Ain.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Section 50
notes: Court has no power to exempt defendant from interest liability; only the financial institution itself can waive interest on its loans

appellant: Jafri Soap and Chemical Industries and others
facts_summary: Petitioner sought remedy outside the Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 framework challenging orders in Artha Rin proceedings. Court examined whether parties could bypass the mandatory provisions of the Ain.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 19, 20, 41, 42
notes: No court can entertain application ignoring mandatory Artha Rin Adalat Ain provisions; parties cannot bypass statutory remedies

respondent: Government of Bangladesh, Ministry of Primary and Mass Education and others
facts_summary: Nationalized primary school teachers challenged rules under the 2013 Service Conditions Rules that reduced their effective service years by 50% and created discriminatory seniority provisions favouring direct recruits over nationalized teachers, affecting their promotions and time scales.
statutes_cited: অধিগ্রহণকৃত বেসরকারি প্রাথমিক বিশ্ববিদ্যালয়ের শিক্ষক (চাকুরি শর্তাবলী নির্ধারণ) বিধিমালা ২০১৩ Rules 2(Ga) and 9(1)
notes: Rule 9(1) of 2013 Rules struck down as manifestly unreasonable and self-contradictory; nationalized teachers' seniority rights protected

facts_summary: Candidate who successfully passed all four stages of BCS examination was recommended by the Bangladesh Public Service Commission but the appropriate government agency refused appointment without showing any valid or lawful reason.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Civil Service Recruitment Rules 1981 Rule 4(3)(A)(B); Constitution
notes: Refusing appointment of BPSC-recommended candidate without valid cause declared unlawful and unconstitutional

respondent: Secretary, Ministry of Civil Aviation, Bangladesh and others
facts_summary: Biman Bangladesh Airlines employee challenged promotion decisions that were based solely on interview performance, disregarding accumulated ACR service records over years. This practice created scope for arbitrariness and unlawful decisions.
statutes_cited: Service promotion rules; ACR-based evaluation principles
notes: Promotion solely by interview found unfair; petitioner deprived unlawfully and deserves retrospective promotion

facts_summary: Government school and college teachers challenged Nitimala 2012 prohibiting engagement in coaching business, arguing it was unconstitutional and that the Anti-Corruption Commission had no jurisdiction to inquire into their coaching activities.
statutes_cited: Public Servant (Discipline and Appeal) Rules 1985; Penal Code 1860 Section 166; Anti-Corruption Commission Act 2004; Nitimala 2012
notes: Nitimala upheld as valid supplement to existing service rules; teachers already prohibited from coaching by their service rules

respondent: Government of Bangladesh, Ministry of Post Telecommunications and IT and others
facts_summary: Government employees employed temporarily for 10-15 years challenged an executive order amendment affecting their regularization. The authority had extended their employment consistently over many years, creating a legitimate expectation of permanent absorption.
statutes_cited: Legitimate expectation doctrine; Constitutional rights
notes: Employees' legitimate expectation of absorption upheld after years of continuous service; executive order amendment found whimsical and beyond authority

facts_summary: Government temporary contingent staff who had served for 10-15 years sought regularization of their posts. The authority had been satisfied with their work and recommended regularization but failed to act on the recommendation.
statutes_cited: Legitimate expectation doctrine; service regularization principles
notes: Long-serving temporary government employees cannot be denied expectation of regularization especially when their work was satisfactory

facts_summary: Doctor petitioner lost seniority despite making application for promotion before a colleague who was subsequently promoted. The authority failed to process petitioner's application in time, resulting in unjust loss of seniority through no fault of the petitioner.
statutes_cited: Service promotion rules; principles of natural justice and equity
notes: Seniority ordered to be restored from date of colleague's promotion; writ court as court of equity cannot allow unjust loss of seniority

appellant: Md. Yousuf Ali Akon and others
facts_summary: Petitioners were appointed as Master Pilots on probation with an express promise in the BIWTA advertisement that they would be permanently absorbed after satisfactory completion of one year probation. BIWTA failed to absorb them despite satisfactory service.
statutes_cited: Service regulations; legitimate expectation doctrine
notes: Petitioners had legitimate expectation of absorption based on express promise in advertisement; BIWTA directed to permanently absorb them

facts_summary: A Steno-Typist was promoted to Stenographer and claimed entitlement to a fresh slot of time scales after promotion. The question was whether benefits drawn as Steno-Typist prevented entitlement to time scales in the promoted post of Stenographer.
statutes_cited: Service rules; time scale regulations; promotion benefits
notes: Promoted Stenographer entitled to fresh time scales in new post; previous time scales as Steno-Typist do not bar entitlement in promoted post

facts_summary: Case clarifying the meaning of a person in the service of the Republic for Administrative Tribunal jurisdiction, specifically whether the definition includes persons who have been retired, dismissed, removed, or discharged from service.
statutes_cited: Administrative Tribunal Act; Constitution
notes: Retired, dismissed and discharged government servants fall within Administrative Tribunal jurisdiction for service-related grievances

appellant: Gazi A.K.M. Fazlul Haque and others
facts_summary: Privatization Commission officers challenged promotion criteria and process. Court examined whether seniority alone was sufficient for promotion and what role merit and selection committees played in the promotion process for Deputy Directors seeking promotion to Director.
statutes_cited: Privatization Commission Officers and Employees Service Regulations 2002; Constitution Article 102
notes: Both seniority and merit required for promotion; petitioners entitled to be considered for promotion under proper criteria including minimum 5 years service

facts_summary: Petitioners challenged disciplinary action taken against them without issuing proper show cause notice as required under service rules and without forming a three-member enquiry committee to dispose of disciplinary proceedings within 60 days as mandated.
statutes_cited: Government service disciplinary rules; show cause notice requirement Rule 5.4
notes: Disciplinary action set aside; mandatory show cause notice and enquiry committee requirements under service rules were not followed

respondent: Bangabandhu Sheikh Mujib Medical University (BSMMU) and others
facts_summary: Petitioner challenged an appointment at BSMMU alleging the selection committee abused its power in violation of Article 31 of the Constitution. The case raised whether unlawful appointments could be validated by the passage of time and rendering of service in the post.
statutes_cited: Constitution of Bangladesh Article 31; BSMMU appointment rules
notes: Court held unlawful appointments cannot be validated by service; selection committee abuse of power is ground for setting aside interview result

facts_summary: Land dispute in Chittagong Hill Tracts involving declaration of a deed and mutation case. Court examined mandatory notice requirements to statutory authorities under CHT Regulation 1900 before filing suits, and the binding nature of CHT customary laws.
statutes_cited: Chittagong Hill Tracts Regulation 1900; Registration Act 1908; Constitution Article 152
notes: Case establishes mandatory notice requirement in CHT land suits; outcome for specific parties not determinable from available ratio

facts_summary: Petitioners challenged a decision of the university Syndicate that affected their rights. Court found the Syndicate had grossly violated well-settled principles of natural justice in taking a prejudicial decision without acting fairly and in accordance with law.
statutes_cited: Principles of natural justice; administrative fairness doctrine
notes: Syndicate order vitiated as gross violation of natural justice; failure to act fairly leads to arbitrariness which vitiates the impugned order

facts_summary: Plaintiff sought permanent injunction over property where defendant claimed title based on City Corporation tax receipts without a legitimate chain of title. Court identified defendant's claim as that of a land grabber whose deeds did not refer to any specific immovable property.
statutes_cited: Code of Civil Procedure; property title and chain of ownership
notes: City Corporation tax receipt alone not proof of possession; land grabber's claim rejected as isolated from any valid chain of title

facts_summary: Petitioner challenged an order rejecting their application to dismiss a plaint on grounds of res judicata. The suit land was declared as forest by a Gazette notification in 1952 and subsequently acquired by the Government under the State Acquisition and Tenancy Act. The Forest Department later attempted additional declarations which the Court found unnecessary.
statutes_cited: Forest Act 1927; State Acquisition and Tenancy Act Sections 3(2) and 20(2a)(iii)
notes: Rule discharged; Gazette notification declaring land as forest sufficient to determine character without additional Forest Act declaration

facts_summary: International commodity trading company sought appointment of an arbitrator from the respondent Vietnamese state corporation for formation of an arbitration tribunal. The respondent denied the existence of any arbitration agreement. Parties communicated through a broker whose status was disputed. Court examined whether email communications created contractual obligations.
statutes_cited: Arbitration Act 2001 Sections 9, 12, 17
notes: Rule discharged; no arbitration agreement found between parties; email communications did not create contractual obligation

facts_summary: Petitioner challenged an interlocutory order passed in an Arbitration Miscellaneous Case rejecting their application to call for records of arbitration proceedings from the arbitrators. Key question was whether civil revision is maintainable against such interlocutory orders under the Arbitration Act 2001.
statutes_cited: Arbitration Act 2001 Section 42; Code of Civil Procedure Section 115
notes: Rule discharged; civil revision maintainable but the application was unnecessary as petitioner could obtain records from his own arbitrator

appellant: Prof. Dr. Muhammad Yunus and others
facts_summary: Petitioners challenged charge framing order against them under the Bangladesh Labour Act 2006 at Grameen Telecom. Labour Inspector found violations including failure to make employees permanent after probation, failure to grant annual leave with pay, and failure to constitute Labour Participation Fund and Labour Welfare Fund.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Labour Act 2006 Sections 303(Uma), 307, 117, 314; Labour Welfare Foundation Law 2006
notes: Rule discharged; charge framing order upheld; limitation question is mixed question of law and fact requiring evidence; nonprofit company still liable for labor welfare contributions

facts_summary: Pre-emptors filed pre-emption case claiming land was sold behind their back. Evidence showed that the vendors had approached the pre-emptors before the sale to sell the land but the pre-emptors refused and advised the sale to the current pre-emptee. This conduct constituted waiver of pre-emption rights.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950 Section 96; Registration Act 1908 Section 60; Non-Agricultural Tenancy Act 1949 Section 24
notes: Rule discharged; pre-emptors waived pre-emption right through their conduct before and after the sale

respondent: Judge, Artha Rin Adalat, Chattogram and others
facts_summary: Petitioner guarantor filed writ petition against a civil detention order issued in an Artha Rin execution case without the bank filing the application properly under Section 34. Court distinguished between accused persons and judgment debtors, holding that judgment debtors need not surrender to court like criminal accused.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 2, 4, 5, 6(1), 6(5), 34, 41, 44
notes: Rule made absolute; civil detention order quashed as bank did not file application under Section 34 with proper verification or affidavit

facts_summary: Chattogram Port Authority challenged a suit filed against it for land dispute without service of prior notice under Section 49 of the Chittagong Port Ordinance 1976. Port Authority argued the suit was not maintainable for non-service of notice. Court held that after joining issue, plaint cannot be rejected and service of summons satisfied the notice requirement.
statutes_cited: Chittagong Port Ordinance 1976 Section 49; Code of Civil Procedure Order VII Rule 2
notes: Rule discharged; Port Authority's challenge failed; service of summons with plaint equivalent to required notice where no alternative remedy exists for land disputes

facts_summary: Petitioner challenged lower court order condoning 188-day delay in filing an application to set aside an arbitral award under Section 42 of the Arbitration Act 2001. Question was whether Section 5 of the Limitation Act applies to condone delay in arbitral award challenge proceedings.
statutes_cited: Arbitration Act 2001 Section 42; Limitation Act 1908 Sections 5 and 29
notes: Rule made absolute; Section 5 of Limitation Act not applicable to special law with specific limitation period; lower court order condoning delay reversed

facts_summary: Plaintiffs filed suit for declaration that a registered sale deed was forged, arguing the document was not read out to illiterate parties before execution. Trial court decreed the suit but appellate court reversed. High Court found plaintiffs failed to discharge their burden of proof to rebut the presumption of valid registration.
statutes_cited: Registration Act 1908 Sections 31, 32, 34, 35; Evidence Act 1872 Sections 101, 103; Specific Relief Act Section 42
notes: Rule discharged; registered deed carries strong presumptive evidence of valid execution; plaintiff failed to rebut presumption

facts_summary: Biman Bangladesh Airlines employee challenged compulsory retirement ordered by the CEO/Managing Director without any board of directors resolution. The Articles of Association required a board resolution for retirement decisions. No delegated authority existed for the CEO to take this decision independently.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Biman Corporation Employee Regulations 1988; Bangladesh Biman Corporation Ordinance 1977; Articles of Association
notes: Rule found merit; CEO's retirement order declared arbitrary and without jurisdiction for lack of board resolution and delegated authority

facts_summary: Petitioner company sought injunction under the Arbitration Act 2001 restraining disposal of property pending arbitration. Respondent denied existence of arbitration agreement and applied for handwriting expert examination of disputed signatures. Lower court allowed the handwriting expert application.
statutes_cited: Arbitration Act 2001 Sections 7A, 10, 17(ka), 19, 32; Evidence Act 1872 Section 45
notes: Rule made absolute; lower court exceeded limited powers under Section 7K; power to examine handwriting expert vested only in arbitral tribunal under Section 17(ka)

appellant: Probir Kumar Dey alias Saiful and another
facts_summary: Partition suit over 4.81 decimals of land after owner's death. Hindu widow claimed same share as son under the Hindu Women's Rights to Property Act 1937. One son converted to Islam before suit was filed. Court examined rights of widow, converted son, and unmarried daughter to inheritance and partition.
statutes_cited: Hindu Women's Rights to Property Act 1937 Section 3; Caste Disability Removal Act 1850; Dayabhaga school of Hindu Law
notes: Plaintiffs entitled to partition; widow has same rights as son; converted son lost right to father's property; unmarried daughter entitled to property

facts_summary: Prof. Yunus and GTC challenged charge framing order in Labour Court for alleged violations of Bangladesh Labour Act 2006 including not making probationary employees permanent, not granting annual leave, and not constituting Labour Welfare Fund. Petitioner argued as Nobel laureate he had no role in financial management.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Labour Act 2006 Sections 303(Uma), 307, 117, 314; Labour Welfare Foundation Law 2006
notes: Rule discharged; charge framing upheld; Board of Directors responsible for GTC administration; nonprofit company not exempt from Labour Welfare Fund contribution

facts_summary: Certificate debtor challenged certificate case proceedings initiated for a defaulted loan. The Certificate Officer started proceedings without proper objective assessment of whether the outstanding dues claimed by the bank were actually payable, acting only on an improperly filed requisition letter.
statutes_cited: Public Demands Recovery Act 1913 Sections 4, 6, 16; Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Section 5(5)
notes: Certificate proceeding quashed; Certificate Officer acted without objective satisfaction about actual outstanding dues

facts_summary: Associate Professor of Dhaka University challenged demotion to Assistant Professor by University Syndicate for alleged plagiarism. The Syndicate punished her without following mandatory procedures — no formal charge was framed, no copy of enquiry report was provided, and no meaningful opportunity to defend was given.
statutes_cited: Enquiry Committee and Tribunal Regulations 1980 Regulation 7(a); Dhaka University Order 1973 Article 52; First Statutes of Dhaka University
notes: Syndicate decision declared illegal; mandatory charge framing and opportunity to be heard not followed; decision vitiated by bias and malafide

facts_summary: Plaintiffs filed title suit alleging forged documents, claiming their father was bedridden, blind, and incapacitated from 1980 until death and could not have signed the disputed deeds. Defendants denied this. Trial court decreed for plaintiffs; appellate court reversed.
statutes_cited: Evidence Act 1872 Sections 101, 103; Registration Act 1908; Limitation Act 1908 Article 120
notes: Suit set aside; plaintiff failed to discharge burden of proof; suit also barred by limitation as cause of action arose earlier than claimed

facts_summary: Former Additional District Judge challenged Rule 300(a) of Bangladesh Service Rules Part I which forfeited pension upon resignation from service. Petitioner had nineteen years of unblemished service and applied for pension after resignation, which was denied by the Comptroller and Auditor General.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Service Rules Part I Rule 300; Constitution of Bangladesh Articles 27, 31
notes: Rule 300(a) on pension forfeiture upon resignation declared ultra vires the Constitution; person who resigns is entitled to pension based on length of service

facts_summary: Joint venture petitioner was declared final responsive tenderer for Bangladesh Railway online ticketing system. A competitor filed a complaint before the Review Panel alleging evaluation irregularities. Review Panel allowed the complaint and recommended re-tender. Petitioner challenged the decision as the complaint was filed out of time.
statutes_cited: Public Procurement Act 2006 Sections 29, 30; Public Procurement Rules 2008 Rules 56, 57, 60
notes: Review Panel decision set aside; respondent's complaint was barred by limitation as filed beyond the 7-day period; Review Panel failed to address limitation

facts_summary: Suit for cancellation of a Nadabinama deed. Plaintiffs claimed the deed was not read out to them before their signatures were obtained, and they were misled into believing it was a partition deed. The defendants are literate and plaintiffs are illiterate rural people.
statutes_cited: Evidence Act 1872 Section 102; Limitation Act 1908 Section 18; Registration Act 1908
notes: Rule discharged upholding deed cancellation; reading out a deed to illiterate executants before execution is a custom having force of law

facts_summary: Auction purchasers of RAJUK land obtained title through court execution proceedings confirmed by Appellate Division. ACC then issued notices against them alleging evasion of registration fees. Petitioners challenged the notices as mala fide since the auction purchase was judicially confirmed.
statutes_cited: Anti-Corruption Commission Act 2004 Sections 19, 20; ACC Rules 2007 Rules 3(5) and 20; Registration Act 1908 Section 63A
notes: ACC notices quashed; evasion of registration fees for court-confirmed auction sale does not constitute scheduled offence under ACC Act; notices issued mala fide

facts_summary: Managing Director and shareholder of a Borrower-Company challenged Bangladesh Bank notices threatening to vacate his directorship at Mercantile Bank due to failure to repay rescheduled loan down payment as guarantor of the Borrower-Company.
statutes_cited: Banking Companies Act 1991 Section 17(8); Companies Act 1994 Section 43; Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 3, 5, 6
notes: Petition dismissed; directorship vacates when director is defaulter guarantor; distinction between defaulter loanee and defaulter guarantor does not protect directorship

respondent: Court of 1st Joint District Judge and Artha Rin Adalat and another
facts_summary: Bank obtained certificate of ownership in Artha Rin execution case but did not take physical possession. After execution case was disposed, judgment-debtor applied to redeem mortgaged property by depositing outstanding dues. Bank argued executing court became functus officio after disposing the case.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 20, 33(1), 33(4), 33(7), 57; right of redemption; foreclosure
notes: Rule discharged; court not functus officio as possession never handed over; right of redemption survives where auction not properly conducted

facts_summary: Petitioners challenged inclusion of their property in 1986 Gazette as abandoned property. Evidence showed Government never took possession of the property, accepted land tax from petitioners until 2015, and RAJUK issued construction permission. Government failed to show any record of possession or maintenance as required for abandoned property designation.
statutes_cited: Abandoned Buildings (Supplementary Provisions) Ordinance 1985 Section 5(1)(a); P.O. 16 of 1972
notes: Rule made absolute; property not abandoned as Government never took possession; mandatory precondition of possession not satisfied

facts_summary: Civil appeal arising from title suit over 7 decimal land. Appellate court examined oral and documentary evidence including CS khatian and other records. High Court assessed whether lower court correctly evaluated documentary evidence under Evidence Act.
statutes_cited: Evidence Act 1872 Sections 65 and 115; Civil Rules and Orders Chapter 17
notes: Appeal dismissed; trial court decree for plaintiff upheld; documentary evidence properly assessed including CS khatian accepted as exhibit

respondent: Advocate Md. Mozammel Haque and others
facts_summary: Rent control ejectment suit against a monthly tenant. Trial court decreed ejectment finding tenant a defaulter based on an answer given in cross-examination to an extraneous question outside the pleadings. High Court found this erroneous and also noted House Rent Control Act does not allow eviction for a son's use of premises.
statutes_cited: House Rent Control Act 1991 Section 18; Code of Civil Procedure
notes: Trial court judgment set aside; admission must be clear and unambiguous; plaintiff failed to prove grounds for ejectment

facts_summary: Plaintiff purchased and mutated suit land in 1991 and was in possession until dispossessed by the defendant, a subsequent purchaser, in 1997. Trial Court decreed the suit in part, which was reversed by the First Appellate Court on the ground that partition was not sought. High Court Division restored the Trial Court decree. Appellate Division found that prior possession and dispossession having been proved, no prayer for partition was necessary.
statutes_cited: Code of Civil Procedure; Transfer of Property Act; title and possession
notes: Appeal dismissed; plaintiff who proved title, prior possession and dispossession can sue for recovery without also praying for partition

facts_summary: Earlier Appellate Division decision held that a disputed RAJUK plot was not situated in the CS and SA plot as claimed by respondent No.5 and had not been released from acquisition. Despite this, respondent filed a fresh title suit. Appellant filed a writ petition seeking delivery of possession and execution of lease deed. High Court Division discharged the writ, but Appellate Division found this breached Article 111 as the respondent's title claim was barred by res judicata.
statutes_cited: Constitution of Bangladesh Article 111; res judicata; land acquisition proceedings
notes: High Court Division committed illegality in discharging the writ; respondent's title claim over disputed plot already decided against him in earlier proceedings; barred by res judicata

respondent: Professor M. Shamsul Alam and others
facts_summary: Niko Resources obtained gas exploration rights in Bangladesh through a corrupt scheme involving payment of gratification to secure the Joint Venture Agreement and Gas Purchase and Sale Agreement. High Court Division declared both agreements without lawful authority. Appellate Division upheld this finding and confirmed attachment of assets.
statutes_cited: Contract Act 1872 Section 23; Penal Code Sections 161, 162, 163; UNCAC Articles 31, 51, 53, 54
notes: Petition dismissed; contracts procured by corruption void ab initio; no one can benefit from their own wrong; JVA and gas sale agreement declared without lawful authority

respondent: Sk. Md. Abdullah Faruque and others
facts_summary: High Court Division directed absorption of nationalized college teachers as Lecturers in government colleges relying on the 2018 Absorption Rules, although the writ petitioners had not prayed for absorption under those rules and the Rule Nisi had not been issued in that specific term. Appellate Division held the HCD travelled beyond the scope of the Rule Nisi.
statutes_cited: Constitution Article 102; Nationalized College Teachers Absorption Rules 2018; Supreme Court HCD Rules 1973 Chapter XIA
notes: HCD judgment set aside; court cannot grant relief beyond what was prayed for in the writ petition or the terms of the Rule Nisi

respondent: Secretary, Ministry of Civil Aviation and others
facts_summary: Biman Bangladesh Airlines employee challenged a promotion order on the ground that Biman's promotion process relied solely on interview performance without considering Annual Confidential Reports accumulated over years of service. This created scope for arbitrariness and unlawful decisions in promotion.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Biman promotion rules; principles of fair promotion procedure
notes: Court found petitioner was deprived unlawfully by an unfair method of promotion selection and deserved to be promoted; Biman directed to address the lacuna in its promotion procedure

facts_summary: Petitioner was awarded a government tender but the NOC was not issued to them. The Review Panel exceeded its jurisdiction in reconsidering the tender evaluation. The authority's failure to issue the NOC despite the award to the petitioner was found to be manifestly arbitrary and without lawful authority.
statutes_cited: Public Procurement Rules 2008 Rule 60; Central Procurement Technical Unit rules
notes: Review Panel exceeded jurisdiction; failure to issue NOC was arbitrary and without lawful authority; a tender award is meaningless if NOC is withheld

respondent: Government of Bangladesh, Ministry of Expatriates Welfare and others
facts_summary: Petitioner's emigration recruiting license was cancelled along with forfeiture of securities. The authority issued the cancellation order without giving the petitioner any opportunity of being heard before the cancellation, in violation of Section 14(1) of the Emigration Ordinance 1982 which mandates a hearing before cancellation.
statutes_cited: Emigration Ordinance 1982 Section 14
notes: Order of cancellation was bad in law; mandatory requirement of opportunity of being heard before cancellation of license was not followed

appellant: Monto Sheikh being dead his legal heirs
respondent: Ibrahim Miah being dead his legal heirs
facts_summary: Plaintiffs filed suit for declaration of title and possession over suit land. Their claim of title was based on a settlement which had subsequently been cancelled, with the cancellation order remaining in existence. Plaintiffs failed to prove both title and possession in the suit land.
statutes_cited: Evidence Act 1872; Code of Civil Procedure; title and possession
notes: Plaintiff could not prove title or possession; the settlement which was the basis of their title claim had been cancelled

respondent: Government of Bangladesh, Ministry of Post Telecommunications and IT and others
facts_summary: Petitioners were temporary employees whose tenure was extended by the authority either expressly or by conduct over a period of time. An executive order amendment was challenged as whimsical and beyond authority. Court found that where executives permit temporary employment to extend over time, the employees can legitimately expect to be absorbed into permanent service.
statutes_cited: Administrative law; principles of legitimate expectation; executive authority
notes: Executive order amendment found whimsical and beyond authority; employees who are permitted to continue beyond temporary period have legitimate expectation of absorption

facts_summary: In an execution proceeding, the judgment debtors had paid Tk. 62,50,000 to the decree holder bank during pendency of the suit which was not adjusted by the bank when filing the execution case. The executing court adjusted this amount but in doing so went beyond the decree. The bank challenged the executing court's order as beyond its jurisdiction.
statutes_cited: Code of Civil Procedure; execution of money decrees; limits of executing court jurisdiction
notes: Executing court's order set aside as it travelled beyond the decree; executing court can adjust amounts paid during execution pendency but cannot go beyond the decree itself

respondent: Lakkatura Tea Company Ltd and others
facts_summary: Plaintiff's plaint was rejected by invoking Section 52A of the Registration Act and Section 53C of the Transfer of Property Act. The plaintiff's name was erroneously not inserted in the Record of Rights. Court held that Record of Right alone does not confer title but has presumptive value, and a person whose name was erroneously omitted can seek a declaration from court.
statutes_cited: Registration Act Section 52A; Transfer of Property Act Section 53C; Code of Civil Procedure Order VII Rule 11
notes: Plaint rejection overturned; plaintiff made out a distinct cause that should be adjudicated; inherent jurisdiction cannot be invoked to reject the plaint at inception

facts_summary: Petitioners challenged disciplinary action taken by Islamic University against them. The authority failed to follow due process — it did not inform them of the charges and proposed punishment at the initial stage, and did not afford them an opportunity to file a reply before taking action against them.
statutes_cited: Constitution of Bangladesh Article 102(2); principles of due process and natural justice
notes: Non-compliance with due process elements is fatal to authority's subsequent action; both informing of charge and opportunity to reply are mandatory before disciplinary action

respondent: Chairman, First Labour Court, Dhaka and others
facts_summary: Grameenphone challenged Labour Court jurisdiction over workers engaged through outsourcing arrangements. The court examined the legal nature of outsourcing contracts in Bangladesh — whether outsourced workers are direct employees of the service recipient or employees of the contractor.
statutes_cited: Labour Act 2006; outsourcing contract principles
notes: Outsourcing is a contract of services not employment; service recipient not the employer of persons engaged by the contractor; Labour Court jurisdiction does not extend to outsourced workers

respondent: Charge Officer and Appeal Officer and others
facts_summary: Revenue officer acting as Settlement Officer invoked Rule 42 of the Settlement Rules to nullify the course of record-of-rights proceedings and send them back for hearing afresh. Court found that when parliament has provided specific forums and sequential steps for record-of-rights disputes up to the Appellate Division, a revenue officer cannot nullify this entire process.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Rules 1955 Rule 42; record of rights legislation
notes: Settlement Officer's purported nullification of record-of-rights proceedings declared a nullity; government itself has no power to nullify such proceedings and cannot delegate such power to a revenue officer

facts_summary: Petitioners who served as temporary contingent staff for 10 to 15 years sought regularization of their posts. The authority had been satisfied with their work and the superior authority had recommended regularization. The court examined whether long-serving temporary employees have a legitimate expectation of regularization.
statutes_cited: Administrative law; legitimate expectation doctrine
notes: While regularization cannot be claimed as of right, after 10-15 years of service with superior authority's recommendation, employees have legitimate expectation of regularization

facts_summary: Authority cancelled the lease of petitioners over case land and in the same action called upon them to appear with valid papers on a future date. The authority cancelled the lease first without giving petitioners an opportunity to be heard beforehand, making the hearing post-decisional rather than pre-decisional.
statutes_cited: Principles of natural justice; audi alteram partem; duty to act fairly
notes: Lease cancellation procedure violated natural justice; hearing must be pre-decisional not post-decisional; authority must hear party first and then decide, not cancel first and invite papers afterward

facts_summary: Suit for specific performance of a contract for sale based on an affidavit/bainapatra dated 03.04.1995. The plaintiff neither registered the contract within six months of Section 17B of the Registration Act coming into force on 1st July 2005, nor instituted a suit for specific performance within six months after that period expired.
statutes_cited: Registration Act 1908 Section 17B; Code of Civil Procedure Order 7 Rule 11
notes: Plaint rejected; contract for sale became void after failure to register or file suit within prescribed periods under Section 17B of Registration Act

facts_summary: Petitioner filed a writ petition against a decree or post-decree order of the Artha Rin Adalat instead of using the statutory appellate forum. Court held that since the Artha Rin Adalat Ain is a special law with an overriding provision and prescribed special procedure, a party aggrieved by its decree must use the appeal mechanism, not writ jurisdiction.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 6, 19, 41
notes: Writ petition not maintainable against Artha Rin decree; special law prescribes special procedure and appellate forum which must be exhausted first

appellant: Shetu International Pvt. Ltd and others
respondent: Artha Rin Adalat-2, Dhaka and another
facts_summary: In an Artha Rin proceeding, the plaintiff-bank published summons in a newspaper at its own instance without following proper procedure — no summons was served through Process Server or by post, and the court had passed no order authorizing newspaper publication. The plaintiff acted on its own motion without court authorization.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 6(2) and 7(1)
notes: Newspaper publication of summons by plaintiff at its own instance without court order is contrary to Section 7(1) of the Ain; proper procedure requires Process Server service and court order before newspaper publication

facts_summary: Partition suit between co-sharers over disputed property. Defendants had possession and control of the suit land. Trial court appointed a receiver under Order 40 Rule 1 of the CPC to protect the property pending final determination. Defendants appealed the appointment of receiver.
statutes_cited: Code of Civil Procedure 1908 Order 40 Rule 1; partition suit provisions
notes: Receiver appointment upheld; prima facie danger to property established; defendants in possession might alienate mesne-profits and deprive plaintiff sisters of their rights pending final decree

facts_summary: Power company sought to invoke writ jurisdiction to challenge a contract with BPDB for purchasing electricity on rental basis. Court examined whether the contract was a statutory contract or a commercial contract. Found that BPDB did not enter the contract in exercise of statutory power and it was a purely commercial contract.
statutes_cited: Constitution of Bangladesh Article 102; Arbitration Act 2001; commercial contract principles
notes: Writ petition not maintainable; purely commercial contract between private party and BPDB cannot be challenged through writ jurisdiction; petitioner must seek remedy through contractual/civil forums

facts_summary: Petitioner challenged a second Artha Rin execution case filed against him after the first execution case had expired beyond the 6-year period under Section 28(4) of the Ain. Court examined whether the second execution case was barred by limitation where the first case was neither concluded nor disposed of within 6 years.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 28 and 33
notes: Second execution case barred by limitation; Section 28(4) bars new execution case filed after expiry of 6 years from the date of filing of the first execution case

facts_summary: Members of petitioner-samity had a legitimate expectation that Sylhet City Corporation would construct a proposed market after removing sheds from the Bus Terminal, as the Corporation had made a commitment to do so. The Corporation failed to honor this commitment.
statutes_cited: Administrative law; legitimate expectation doctrine; municipal authority obligations
notes: Legitimate expectation should be fulfilled; City Corporation Authority made a clear commitment to petitioner-samity and is bound by it

facts_summary: Title dispute where one party held a registered kabala deed dated 13.05.1965 while the other relied on record of rights. Court examined the evidentiary value of registered deeds versus record of rights entries in determining title to land.
statutes_cited: Evidence Act 1872 Section 90; Registration Act 1908
notes: Registered kabala prevails over record of rights; registered deed over 30 years old from proper custody presumed genuine under Section 90 of Evidence Act; record of right alone does not confer title

facts_summary: Government invited tender for land, petitioner participated and the tender was accepted. After acceptance and payment of earnest money as stipulated, the government attempted to change the consideration value. Petitioner challenged this through writ jurisdiction since the government was a party.
statutes_cited: Contract Act 1872; offer and acceptance principles; legitimate expectation
notes: Offer and acceptance complete upon tender and payment of earnest money; government cannot change consideration after acceptance; property passes to buyer when sale becomes binding

facts_summary: Petitioner entered into power purchase contracts with BPDB containing arbitration clauses specifying arbitration under the Arbitration Act 2001. Petitioner tried to invoke writ jurisdiction to interpret and challenge the contracts instead of using the arbitration mechanism agreed upon.
statutes_cited: Arbitration Act 2001 Section 7; Constitution of Bangladesh Article 102
notes: Writ not maintainable; Section 7 of Arbitration Act restricts judicial intervention in matters covered by arbitration agreement; petitioner who signed contract with open eyes must use arbitration

respondent: Chairman, 1st Court of Settlement and another
facts_summary: Property was declared abandoned under P.O. 16 of 1972. Petitioners or their vendor were not in possession of the property at the relevant time, only entering possession in 1984. Petitioners held a decree for specific performance of contract but challenged the abandoned property designation.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Abandoned Property Order P.O. 16 of 1972 Article 7; Abandoned Buildings Supplementary Provision Ordinance 1985 Section 5
notes: Abandoned property designation stands; petitioners not in possession at relevant time; decree for specific performance does not determine the abandoned character of property

facts_summary: Petitioner faced both criminal proceedings under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act and execution proceedings in the Artha Rin Adalat over the same loan transaction. This created a situation where the petitioner would have to deposit 50% under both proceedings, together almost covering the full claimed amount, leading to potential unjust enrichment of the creditor.
statutes_cited: Negotiable Instruments Act 1881 Section 138; Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Section 41; Code of Criminal Procedure Section 344
notes: Criminal proceedings stayed pending Artha Rin suit; double proceedings over same loan transaction constitutes absurd inconvenience; ends of justice demand single forum for same transaction

facts_summary: Partition suit involving legal heirs who had not been impleaded as parties. Petitioners argued they would be prejudiced by not being formally added. Court examined whether non-impleaded legal heirs could still protect their interests in the partitioned property.
statutes_cited: Code of Civil Procedure 1908 Order 40 Rule 1; partition suit provisions
notes: Non-impleaded legal heirs not prejudiced; as heirs they are entitled to shares of their predecessors; even non-contesting parties can pray for allotment of saham on payment of court fees before drawing up final decree

facts_summary: Pre-emption case where the pre-emptee claimed protection under sub-section 10 of Section 96 of the State Acquisition and Tenancy Act as a relation within three degrees by consanguinity. Court examined the table of kindred under Schedule 1 of the Indian Succession Act 1925 and found that a brother-in-law does not fall within three degrees by consanguinity.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950 Section 96(10); Indian Succession Act 1925 Section 28 and Schedule 1
notes: Pre-emptee cannot claim protection; brother-in-law not within three degrees by consanguinity; pre-emption case proceeds

facts_summary: Petitioner filed a civil court suit challenging an action or order of the Settlement Officer during revisional survey under Section 144 of the SAT Act before final publication of record-of-rights. Court held that Section 144B bars all civil court suits during this period.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950 Section 144B; Tenancy Rules Rule 42A
notes: Civil court suit not maintainable during revisional survey period before final publication of record-of-rights; only remedy is Rule 42A of the Tenancy Rules

facts_summary: Sonali Bank challenged a lower court order that purported to exempt the defendant from liability of paying interest on a loan. The bank argued that only the financial institution itself has the exclusive right to exempt any person from payment of interest, not the court.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Section 50
notes: Bank's appeal upheld; court has no power to exempt defendant from interest liability however high the rate; exclusive right to waive interest belongs to the financial institution alone

respondent: Artha Rin Adalat, Dhaka and others
facts_summary: Petitioner was the highest bidder in an Artha Rin auction but failed to deposit the balance 75% bid money within the prescribed period. Instead of directly depositing, petitioner filed an application seeking permission to deposit, thereby voluntarily abandoning the statutory privilege of an auction purchaser.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 33, 38, 45; right of redemption
notes: Petitioner lost statutory privilege by abandonment; by filing application instead of depositing directly, auction purchaser waived all rights to the property and forfeited the earnest money

facts_summary: Petitioner applied for promotion to Personal Professor on 21.12.2003. A colleague who applied later on 28.12.2003 was promoted first on 06.11.2004. Petitioner suffered loss of seniority through no fault of his own. Court exercised writ equity jurisdiction to restore the injustice.
statutes_cited: Constitution of Bangladesh Article 102; natural justice; equity principles in service matters
notes: Seniority restored from date of colleague's promotion; writ court as court of equity can remedy injustice caused by administrative error where petitioner had no fault

appellant: Md. Yousuf Ali Akon and others
facts_summary: BIWTA published an advertisement on 19.01.2004 expressly promising that appointees would be on probation for one year and after satisfactory completion would be permanently absorbed as Master Pilots. Petitioners relied on this express promise, completed probation satisfactorily, but were not absorbed.
statutes_cited: Legitimate expectation doctrine; service appointment rules
notes: Legitimate expectation established from express promise in advertisement; BIWTA bound to absorb petitioners against permanent posts after satisfactory probation completion

respondent: Judge, Artha Rin Adalat and others
facts_summary: Defendant no. 3 in an Artha Rin suit was neither a borrower, nor a guarantor, nor a mortgagor in relation to the loan liability. The bank had joined him as a defendant. Court examined Section 6(5) of the Ain which defines who can be made a necessary party in an Artha Rin suit.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Section 6 and 6(5)
notes: Suit dismissed against petitioner; person who is not borrower, guarantor or mortgagor cannot be made a necessary party in Artha Rin suit under Section 6(5)

respondent: Secretary, Water Resources Ministry and others
facts_summary: Steno-Typist was promoted to Stenographer through open competition based on merit. On promotion to the higher post the petitioner sought entitlement to a new slot of time-scales. Court examined whether earlier time-scales drawn as Steno-Typist affected entitlement to new time-scales as Stenographer.
statutes_cited: Government Service Rules; time-scale provisions for promotion posts
notes: Petitioner entitled to new slot of time-scales on promotion to Stenographer; earlier time-scales as Steno-Typist do not affect new entitlement subject to satisfactory service conditions

facts_summary: A plaint was filed in an Artha Rin Adalat without the mandatory affidavit required under Section 6 of the Ain. The affidavit must accompany the plaint attesting to both the statements made in the plaint and the documents annexed. Non-compliance rendered the plaint invalid.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Section 6
notes: Impugned order set aside; plaint without mandatory affidavit is invalid; non-compliance with the mandatory affidavit requirement cannot be sustained in law

facts_summary: Appellate court found a registered gift deed was not acted upon because there was no evidence of delivery of possession to the donee. High Court found this reasoning incorrect and examined the legal validity of a registered Hindu gift deed without delivery of possession.
statutes_cited: Transfer of Property Act 1882 Section 123; Hindu gift law principles
notes: Appellate court's finding reversed; where gift instrument has been registered, delivery of possession is not essential for validity of a gift by a Hindu

respondent: Commissioner, Customs Bond Commissionerate and others
facts_summary: Bond Commissioner issued a show cause notice regarding petitioner's license after NBR had already agreed with a predecessor's proposal. The subsequent Bond Commissioner had no room to deviate from the NBR-approved proposal. Show cause notice was issued while simultaneously requesting other authorities to suspend the license, making it a closed-minded predetermined notice.
statutes_cited: Customs Bond regulations; natural justice principles; administrative fairness
notes: Closed-minded show cause notice declared unlawful; when authority has predetermined the outcome and simultaneously taken adverse action, the notice is a mere formality without legal validity

facts_summary: Government included petitioner's property in the abandoned buildings list without serving any notice upon the owner or occupier under Article 7 of P.O. 16 of 1972 or Section 5(1)(b) of the Ordinance 1985. Petitioner was never asked to show cause or surrender the property.
statutes_cited: Abandoned Buildings (Supplementary Provisions) Ordinance 1985 Section 5(1)(b); P.O. 16 of 1972 Article 7; natural justice
notes: Property not legally abandoned; non-service of mandatory notice disentitles government from claiming legal abandonment; petitioner's right to natural justice violated

appellant: Shoel Textile Mills Ltd and others
facts_summary: Petitioner had their loan rescheduled by the bank. After rescheduling, the bank or another authority attempted to treat the petitioner as a loan defaulter. Court examined Section 5 GaGa of the Bank Companies Act 1991 which defines loan defaulter.
statutes_cited: Bank Companies Act 1991 Sections 5 GaGa and 27 KaKa
notes: Person whose loan has been rescheduled cannot be treated as loan defaulter; rescheduling is the bank's admission that the borrower is not a defaulter under the statutory definition

appellant: Jafri Soap and Chemical Industries and others
facts_summary: Petitioner challenged a proceeding or order of the Artha Rin Adalat by approaching a court or authority outside the provisions of the Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003. Court held that any question regarding Artha Rin proceedings must be raised strictly within the framework of the Ain.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 19, 20, 41, 42
notes: Challenge not maintainable outside the Ain's framework; Section 20 bars any other court from taking cognizance of matters covered by the Artha Rin Adalat Ain

facts_summary: Tenants in suit properties denied the title of the plaintiffs as landlords. Plaintiffs sought eviction but defendants argued that notice under Section 106 of the Transfer of Property Act was required. Court examined whether denial of landlord's title forfeits the tenant's right to notice before eviction.
statutes_cited: Transfer of Property Act Section 106; landlord-tenant relationship; denial of title
notes: Tenants who deny landlord's title forfeit their right to notice under Section 106; notice dispensed with when tenant denies the title of the land lord

facts_summary: Petitioner entered into a bainapatra for land in the Chittagong Hill Tracts. The opposite party received the money, executed the bainapatra and delivered possession. The defendants consented to transfer the land by registered deed through the court. Prior approval for land transfer in CHT is only required for non-inhabitants of the same hill district.
statutes_cited: Rangamati Hill District Local Government Council Act 1989 Section 64; CHT land transfer rules
notes: Specific performance of bainapatra decreed; CHT law does not bar transfer to inhabitant of same hill district; prior approval only needed for non-inhabitants
